1:
The witnesses divide the scene into two pressures and one refusal. The grammatical-historical commentators set the stage with care: this is the first time the mutually hostile Pharisees and Sadducees combine, and Robertson's quip — "hate makes strange bedfellows" — captures the consensus that a shared hatred of Jesus overrode their feud. They identify the "sign from heaven" as something spectacular beyond the earthly miracles He had already given: Samuel's thunder (1 Samuel 12:16-18), Elijah's fire (1 Kings 18:38), or the manna of the wilderness which the Capernaum crowd had demanded (John 6:30-31). Barnes notes the irony that far greater heavenly signs would in fact attend His death.

The Latin and Greek fathers read the red sky allegorically and converge on a two-advent scheme. Augustine, Raban, and the Gloss take the evening red as the blood of Christ's passion at His first coming, by which sin is pardoned, and the lowering morning sky as the fire of judgment at the second; Chrysostom frames it the same way — "now I come as a physician, then as a judge" — earthly signs for this coming, heavenly ones reserved for the next. Jerome and Remigius press the blindness: men who could read the weather could not read the prophets, though "the Lord was in this place, and they knew it not" (as Henry adds). Hilary observes the unusual note that Jesus "left them," a sign that unbelief, not mere departure, was being judged.

The Reformed voices turn it polemical. Calvin and Gill hear in the demand for a fresh sign the very posture of "the Papists of our own day," who treat the gospel as still unproven and call for new miracles though the old are despised. The Wesleyan reading (Clarke, Wesley) is simpler: they wanted a sign Satan could not counterfeit, and were refused all but the sign of Jonah (Matthew 12:39) — the resurrection.

The one genuine division is textual rather than doctrinal: Robertson and Ellicott note that the weather-saying of vv.2-3 is absent from the great manuscripts (Aleph, B), Robertson inclining to reject it and Ellicott defending its originality on the ground of its vivid form. Origen stands a little apart, dwelling less on the sign than on the unlikely alliance, comparing it to Herod and Pilate becoming friends to destroy Jesus (Luke 23:12).
2:
On the weather-saying itself the witnesses are of one mind, and the comment is brief. The grammatical-historical expositors treat it as a proverb drawn from common observation: a red evening sky promises a fair tomorrow, and, as Barnes notes, "almost all nations have observed this" — in Judea the red and lowering morning was the sign of a tempest, though elsewhere the signs of a storm might differ. Robertson lingers on the rare word eudia ("fair weather"), an old poetic term from Zeus as ruler of the air, found on the Rosetta Stone and in a fourth-century papyrus of "calm weather" too still for sailing; and he repeats the manuscript caution that Aleph, B and other authorities omit vv.2-3, McNeile rejecting them, Westcott and Hort bracketing them, though "Jesus often repeated his sayings."

Ellicott, defending the words against omission, draws out their inner force at length. He notes that "sky" obscures the point — Jesus says in effect, "You watch the heaven and are weather-wise as to coming storm or sunshine; if your eyes were open to the signs of the spiritual firmament, you would find tokens enough of the coming sunshine of God's truth." And he presses the detail that the redness is the same in both cases: the flush and excitement pervading men's minds "was at once the prognostic of a brighter day... and the presage of the storm and tempest in which that day should end." He even rebukes Strauss, who called words so suggestive "absolutely unintelligible," as proof that minute criticism can stunt the power of discernment.

The Reformed Calvin calls the comparison "beautiful and highly appropriate." Though the sky is changeable, so that storm and fair weather arise unexpectedly, "the instructions of nature are sufficient to enable men to predict" the day; Christ therefore asks why they do not recognise the kingdom of God when it is made known by signs "not less manifest." Their failure proves they were "excessively occupied with earthly and transitory advantages," blinded "not so much by mistake as by voluntary malice." Gill adds only that Jesus, knowing their views and having wrought signs enough, "thought fit to give them no other answer than this," and Wesley simply points to the parallel in Luke 12:54.
3:
Here the comment turns from the red sky to "the signs of the times," and the schools agree on the indictment. The grammatical-historical voices supply the background. Lightfoot documents how minutely the Jews observed the heavens — at the going out of the feast of Tabernacles they watched the rising smoke to forecast the year's rains, and a wise man at Zippor could "foretell the moistness of the year by the very smell of the dust" — yet they were "dim-sighted at the signs of times," the eminent signs that plainly pointed, "as with the finger," to the days of Messiah now at hand. Robertson glosses the rare word stugnazon (a sky covered with clouds, a lowering gloom), notes it is used elsewhere only of the rich young ruler's sadness (Mark 10:22), and observes how little the Pharisees grasped their situation: "soon Jerusalem would be destroyed and the Jewish state overturned," yet "it is not always easy to discern the signs of our own time."

The Reformed Calvin gives the verse its sharpest application. Men direct their ingenuity to immediate advantage and so read the weather well; "how comes it then that we feel no concern about the signs by which God invites us to himself? Is it not because every man gives himself up to willing indifference, and extinguishes the light which is offered to him?" He turns aside a literalist misreading — Christ does not forbid us to predict weather from the sky, but argues from "the regular course of nature" that those who are acute in earthly matters yet "knowingly and willfully quench the heavenly light by their stupidity" deserve to perish for their ingratitude. He even rebukes those who "believe that it is a mark of prudence purposely to avoid all inquiry into the truth."

The Wesleyan reading (Clarke, Wesley) is direct: the signs of the times are the doctrine and miracles that show "the day-spring from on high has visited you for your salvation," and the gloomy red cloud is the storm of wrath that shall sweep the unbelieving "from the face of the earth." Gill notes the homely realism of the proverb, and Clarke adds a learned aside, quoting Virgil's Georgics on the colours of the sun as weather-omens among the Romans.
4:
The verse repeats Matthew 12:39, and the witnesses note both the repetition and its sting. The grammatical-historical commentators are terse: Robertson calls it "a real doublet," noting the added words "the prophet," and Barnes adds from Mark 8:12 that Jesus "sighed deeply in spirit," "greatly affected with their perverseness and obstinacy." Ellicott observes that as Mark gives it the refusal is more absolute — "no sign shall be given to this generation" — none, that is, of the kind demanded. All agree the one sign granted is the sign of Jonah (Jonah 1:17), which Clarke explains is the resurrection, "typified in the case of Jonah": Christ's earlier miracles had proved His mission, and only this remained "to take away the scandal of his cross and death, to fulfil the Scriptures, and to establish the Christian religion."

On the charge "evil and adulterous generation" the schools converge on the marriage metaphor. The Wesleyan Clarke and Wesley read it as spiritual adultery: Israel, married to the Most High, has played the disloyal wife, "forsaking their true husband, and uniting themselves to Satan and sin"; their "love of the world, which is spiritual adultery," blinds the understanding so that they "would seek no farther sign." The Reformed Calvin agrees the Jews are condemned not for asking a sign as such — "the Lord sometimes permitted his people to do this" — but because they "deliberately provoke God"; the promised sign is that the risen Christ "would in every place cause the voice of his Gospel to be distinctly heard."

The Alexandrian Origen, the chief outlier, develops the adultery image allegorically. The law of the mind is the soul's true husband, given by God as wife to the man "who is law"; the Pharisees and Sadducees, abandoning "the word of truth or the law," "were debauched by falsehood and the law of sin," drawing on Paul's two laws warring in the members (Romans 7). He also insists, against the framing of their question, that the sign of Jonah was itself "a sign from heaven," for the resurrection came from heaven — granted, "out of His own great goodness," even to those who tempted Him, who became also a sign to the robber who entered paradise.
5:
The disciples forget bread, and Jesus warns of "the leaven of the Pharisees and Sadducees." The witnesses agree this is the hinge from the foregoing controversy to a lesson on doctrine. The Latin fathers press the figure of leaven. Jerome makes it false teaching that, like a little leaven leavening the whole lump (1 Corinthians 5:6), spreads "from a small spark into a mighty flame" — the doctrine of "Marcion, Valentinus, and all the heretics" — and he reasons that if the leaven means "corrupt traditions and heretical dogmas," then the loaves with which the multitudes were fed must signify "the true and uncorrupt doctrine." The Gloss and Remigius note the disciples' affectionate poverty, so bound to their Master "that they were unwilling to part from Him for even a moment," that they took "so small care for necessaries" they forgot bread. Hilary warns them not to share "the doctrine of the Jews," since they "on whose times truth itself had chanced should look for no further types of truth."

The Antiochene Chrysostom asks why Jesus spoke obscurely rather than plainly "beware of their doctrine," and answers that He meant "to remind them of those things done in the multiplication of the loaves, knowing them to be forgetful." A blunt charge "would have seemed unreasonable; but to find fault with them on an occasion banished by themselves prepared the way." He stresses the mildness — Christ "makes an excuse in answer Himself," recalling the five loaves, "rousing their attention," drawing them off "Jewish observances" and "raising them out of their little faith."

The grammatical-historical expositors keep to the narrative detail: they had crossed the sea, and Mark notes they had but "one loaf" (Barnes, Ellicott, Robertson). The Reformed Calvin gives the fullest treatment, harmonising Mark's "leaven of Herod" and Luke's "hypocrisy," and turning the warning on his own age: as Pharisees and Sadducees corrupted true piety, so "not only from Popish temples and the dens of sophists and monks does Antichrist vomit out her impostures, but there is a Theology of the Court," and believers must keep "a perpetual Passover with the unleavened bread of sincerity and truth" (1 Corinthians 5:8). The Puritan Henry adds the pastoral note that "disciples are in most danger from hypocrites," against whom they least stand on guard. The Alexandrian Origen reads the crossing spiritually: they passed "from things material to things spiritual," needing "a different kind of loaves" on the other side.
6:
The witnesses treat this short verse mainly by defining the leaven and explaining why the warning took this form. The grammatical-historical commentators all explain that the disciples missed the metaphor because "leaven" was rarely used among the Jews for doctrine. Lightfoot gives two reasons: the figure was commonly applied to "evil affections and the naughtiness of the heart" rather than to teaching ("blessed is that judge who leaveneth his judgment" being about deliberation, not doctrine), and the rabbis had elaborate canons about which leaven — heathen or Cuthite — might lawfully be used, so the disciples naturally heard a rule about bread. Barnes describes the working of leaven as the very image of false doctrine: it passes "secretly, silently, but certainly," insinuating and artful, and the disciples missed his meaning because "no other instance of the use of the word occurs in the Scriptures" for doctrine, while "the Jews had many particular rules about the leaven which might be used in making bread."

Ellicott reconstructs the scene with his usual precision. The form of the warning "was obviously determined by the fact just narrated": the Master "saw the perplexed looks and heard the self-reproaching or mutually accusing whispers of the disciples, and made them the text of a proverb which was a concentrated parable." He notes that Mark's wording is "the leaven of the Pharisees and of Herod," which he takes to be the original, Matthew (or his source) substituting "the better-known Sadducees," since Herod's circle held Sadducean beliefs, and the Sadducean priests, "courting the favour of the Roman rulers," naturally fraternised with the Herodian party (comparing Acts 5:17 and Mark 3:6).

On the nature of the leaven the Wesleyan voices are emphatic. Clarke holds that "bad doctrines act in the soul as leaven does in meal; they assimilate the whole spirit to their own nature," and that "a man's particular creed has a greater influence on his tempers and conduct than most are aware of" — the pride, hypocrisy, and worldly-mindedness of these sects "ruin the major part of the world." Wesley simply names it "their false doctrine," elegantly so called, "for it spreads in the soul, or the Church, as leaven does in meal" (Luke 12:1). Gill notes the occasion was either the disciples' observation that they had forgotten bread or the foregoing encounter with the two parties, the warning arising naturally from both.
7:
The disciples reason that the warning is a reproach for forgetting bread, and the witnesses use the verse to expose the gap between His meaning and theirs. The grammatical-historical voices stress the artless realism of the moment. Robertson calls it "pathetic, the almost jejune inability of the disciples to understand the parabolic warning" so soon after the collision with both parties, and notes the imperfect tense — "they kept it up" — and that the word is "loaves," not "bread." Ellicott finds in their childish self-questioning "a naïveté which testifies to the absolute originality and truthfulness of the record," and so to the genuineness of the question that follows, "which assumes the reality of the two previous miracles." He suggests they may have imagined that, as a Pharisee would not eat bread defiled by the touch of heathen or publican, so their Master forbade them, "however great their need, to receive food at the hands of either of the sects that had combined against Him." Clarke draws the moral that "bodily wants are perceived with the utmost readiness... but the necessities of the soul are rarely discovered, though they are more pressing than those of the body."

The Alexandrian Origen, the outlier, turns the rebuke inward. Noting that "in respect of the reasonings only which we reason within ourselves we are sometimes convicted and reproached as being of little faith," he builds from it a whole doctrine of inward sin: just as a man "commits adultery in his heart only, though not proceeding altogether to the overt act," so he may steal or bear false witness "in his heart only," and "will be punished proportionately to adultery of this kind" — not as one who completed the deed, "but only as he who has done such things in his heart." He even distinguishes the man who, "in addition to willing it, has attempted it, but not carried it out," from him who merely came short of the evil action "in spite of his own will."

The remaining voices keep to the surface sense. Gill takes the disciples to be either puzzled at "what should be the meaning of this caution of Christ's" or "anxiously concerned what they should do for provision," and Wesley voices their thought plainly: "What must we do then for bread, since we have taken no bread with us?" The very crudeness of their reasoning, the expositors agree, is what draws out the rebuke of the verses that follow.
8:
Jesus perceives their reasoning and rebukes their little faith, and the witnesses dwell on both His knowledge and the nature of the fault. The grammatical-historical commentators note that He perceived their thoughts without hearing the debate — Gill ascribes it to "his omniscience," for "he knew the doubts and unbelief, and anxious solicitude of their minds" — and Robertson observes the four pungent questions that follow, with their reference to the baskets of the five thousand (kophinous, Matthew 14:20) and the four thousand (sphyridas, Matthew 15:37), as a measure of "the strain upon the patience of this Teacher of teachers." Ellicott draws a careful distinction: Christ reproves "not the want of discernment which made them slow to receive the meaning of the similitude" — that depended on imaginative power or culture, "for the lack of which they were not responsible" — but "their want of faith," since the memory of how their wants had twice been supplied should have taught them that no such extremity could arise while He was with them.

The Reformed Calvin presses this hardest. After seeing bread "created out of nothing, and in such abundance as to satisfy many thousands of men, and after having seen this done twice, they are now anxious about bread, as if their Master did not always possess the same power" — and this he calls "shameful ingratitude." From it he draws the principle that "all who have once or twice experienced the power of God, and distrust it for the future, are convicted of unbelief; for it is faith that cherishes in our hearts the remembrance of the gifts of God, and faith must have been laid asleep if we allow them to be forgotten."

The Wesleyan Clarke softens the rebuke with comfort: "there are degrees in faith... little faith may be the seed of great faith, and therefore is not to be despised," though many "who should be strong in faith have but a small measure of it" through giving way to sin. Wesley reduces it to a single question: "Am I not able, if need so require, to supply you by a word?"
9:
The witnesses comment briefly here, the verse being part of Christ's appeal to the disciples' memory of the feeding of the five thousand, yet the appeal carries weight in their hands. The grammatical-historical reading (Barnes, who cross-references Matthew 14:19) and Gill agree that the word "understand" points chiefly to the disciples' failure to reckon with the almighty power displayed in the two miraculous feedings: Gill notes it bears both on grasping "the sense of the advice he had now given" and, "rather," on recalling "his almighty power displayed in the two miracles," since "to this the word 'understand' refers." The point, urged without dissent, is that men who had themselves gathered up the fragments — the baskets being kept, as the expositors observe, for memorials, like the preserved manna (Exodus 16:32) — had no excuse for present anxiety about bread.

The Wesleyan Clarke supplies the one developed thought, and turns it into a meditation on providence. It is "astonishing," he writes, "that these men should have any fear of lacking bread, after having seen the two miracles which our blessed Lord alludes to above." Yet so it is: "though men quickly perceive their bodily wants, and are querulous enough till they get them supplied, they as quickly forget the mercy which they had received; and thus God gets few returns of gratitude for his kindnesses." From this he draws the providential lesson that God is sometimes "induced to suffer them often to be in want, and then to supply them in such a way as to prove that their supply has come immediately from the hand of their bountiful Father."

So the very forgetfulness rebuked here becomes, in Clarke's reading, the reason God lets His people taste need: that the deliverance may be unmistakably His own, and gratitude at last awakened. Across all three voices the lesson is single and pressed without controversy — distrust is the fruit of a short memory, and faith lives by remembering what God has already done. The expositors note that Christ does not here work a fresh miracle but recalls the old ones, rousing attention to the past that the disciples may learn to trust for what is to come.
10:
This verse draws only the briefest notice, both witnesses simply identifying the second miraculous feeding to which Jesus appeals — yet the brevity itself is instructive, for the saying is one half of a deliberate pair. Gill paraphrases the recollection warmly: "Have you forgot the other miracle done but a very little while ago, when I fed four thousand men, beside women and children, with seven loaves and a few small fishes?" The grammatical-historical Barnes does no more than cross-reference the earlier narrative (Matthew 15:34), letting the fact carry its own force. The shared and undisputed point is that the seven loaves of the four thousand are set beside the five loaves of the five thousand as a second, confirming proof of a power that made any present anxiety about bread inexcusable.

The witnesses thus treat verses 9 and 10 as a single argument from memory: two distinct feedings, two distinct numbers, two distinct sets of baskets, so that the disciples could not plead a solitary or doubtful wonder. As the expositors note across these verses, Christ's method is not to supply fresh bread on the spot but to recall the bread already given, rousing attention to what is past "that they should have no fear about food."

The reticence of the comment matches the plainness of the lesson. Where the Lord has twice provided out of almost nothing, the remembrance of it ought to silence fear, and the second miracle is named precisely so that the first should not stand alone — a double witness against unbelief. Even the smallest verse in the chapter, then, carries the chapter's recurring theme, the theme the expositors have pressed since verse 8: that the failure of faith is at bottom a failure of memory, and that the cure for "little faith" is to count again the loaves and the baskets that God has already multiplied. What the disciples lacked was not evidence but recollection; the bread of the past, rightly remembered, is itself the food of present faith. The witnesses, sparing of comment here, let the bare repetition of the miracle do its work, confident that a heart which had truly counted the seven loaves and the baskets that remained could never again ask, in the presence of such a Master, where its next meal would be found.
11:
Jesus marvels that they did not perceive He spoke not of bread, and the witnesses dwell on His method of teaching by un-interpreted figure. The grammatical-historical Ellicott captures the consensus: "true to His method of education, our Lord does not Himself interpret the parable, but is content to suggest the train of thought which led to the interpretation"; and the slow-hearted disciples at last followed the clue — "memory did at last quicken faith, and faith imparted the imaginative insight which sees its way through parables and dark sayings." Gill underlines the rebuke at their senselessness: "that you should be so senseless and void of thought, after such instances," as to imagine He was concerned about the bread they had failed to bring.

The Wesleyan Clarke turns it into a lesson on grace and responsibility. "We are not deficient in spiritual knowledge," he writes, "because we have not had sufficient opportunities of acquainting ourselves with God, but because we did not improve the advantages we had. How deep and ruinous must our ignorance be, if God did not give line upon line, precept upon precept, here a little and there a little!" (the cadence of Isaiah 28:10). He and Wesley note that the disciples now perceived the warning was against "the superstition of the Pharisees, which produced hypocrisy, pride, envy," and "the false doctrine of the Sadducees, which denied the existence of a spiritual world, the immortality of the soul, the resurrection of the body, and the providence of God."

The Alexandrian Origen reads the verse within his larger figure of the two breads. Christ, "expounding clearly and representing to them who were being distracted because of the equivocal meaning of loaf and leaven," makes plain that He spoke "not about sensible bread but about the leaven in the teaching." And Origen presses the comfort: so long as the disciples have Jesus with them, fulfilling "Lo, I am with you always unto the consummation of the age" (Matthew 28:20), "we cannot fast nor be in want of food, so that, because of want of it, we should desire to take and eat the forbidden leaven, even from the Pharisees and Sadducees." The abiding presence of Christ is itself the bread that makes the corrupt leaven needless; only when He seems withdrawn does the soul grow hungry enough to crave it.
12:
"Then understood they" — and the witnesses define what the leaven finally proved to be. The grammatical-historical voices are clear. Robertson notes the aorist sunekan, "to grasp, to comprehend": "they saw the point after this elaborate rebuke and explanation that by 'leaven' Jesus meant 'teaching.'" Barnes lists the ways erroneous doctrine resembles leaven — at first "slight and unimportant in appearance," insinuated "unawares and silently," acting "gradually," yet "most certainly," until it "pervades all the soul, and brings in all the faculties under its control." Ellicott sharpens the definition from Luke 12:1, where the leaven is expressly "hypocrisy": the unreality of a life "respectable, rigid, outwardly religious, even earnest in its zeal, and yet wanting in the humility and love which are of the essence of true holiness," while the Sadducean and Herodian leaven was "the more open form of worldliness and self-indulgence" flowing from their denial of the resurrection.

The Reformed Calvin presses the antithesis between "the pure and uncorrupted word of God" and "the inventions which men contrive out of their own brain." Since no doctrine but what comes from God deserves the name of pure, leaven is "every foreign admixture"; faith is "rendered spurious as soon as we are drawn aside from the simplicity of Christ" (2 Corinthians 11:3). He drives it sharply against Rome: as the scribes and priests then governed the Church and mingled their inventions with God's word, so all who do the like "must be rejected, how honourable soever their rank" — "accursed and rebellious is the obedience of those who voluntarily submit to the inventions and laws of the Pope."

One voice stands apart in subject. Jamieson-Fausset-Brown, here commenting on the parallel Nazareth scene, dwells not on leaven but on the townsmen's question "Is not this the carpenter?" (Mark 6:3), reading it as testimony first to Christ's "true and real humanity," for in thirty years His neighbours "had discovered nothing about Him different from other men," and then to "His wonderful humility and self-denial," that being what He was, "He yet never gave any indications of it for thirty years, because His hour was not yet come."
13:
At Caesarea Philippi Jesus asks who men say the Son of Man is, and the witnesses treat both the place and the title as deliberate. The Latin fathers and the Gloss see Him, having drawn His disciples out of "the teaching of the Pharisees," now "laying deep the foundations of the Gospel doctrine," and first taking from their minds "the errors and opinions of others." Jerome explains the city's history — Philip the tetrarch renaming Panaeas "Caesarea in honour of Tiberias Caesar" — and notes that "they who speak of the Son of Man are men, but they who understood His divine nature are called not men but Gods." Raban observes the "remarkable distinction" that the Lord puts forward "the lowliness of the humanity which He had taken upon Him, while His disciple shews the excellence of His divine eternity." Hilary insists the very question implies "something ought to be thought respecting Him beyond what appeared."

The grammatical-historical commentators supply the setting and the title's depth. Gill traces the city's names — Leshem, Dan, Paneas, Caesarea Philippi, distinct from the coastal Caesarea of Acts — and Lightfoot reads "Son of Man" as naming "the seed promised to Adam, the second Adam," carrying "a silent confutation" of the Jews' dreams of an earthly Messiah by recalling the first promise, where "the victory of the promised seed is the bruising of the serpent's head" (Genesis 3:15), not the subduing of kingdoms; he and Henry also hear the title of Daniel 7:13. Henry notes it is at once "a title common to him with others," truly "Man, made of a woman," and the Messianic name humbly chosen, "to show that he was humble."

The Antiochene Chrysostom explains why Jesus led them far from Judea — that, "being set free from all fear, they might say freely what was in their mind" — and why He asked the common people's view rather than the malicious Pharisees', "searching into the minds of the common people, which were not perverted to evil." The Alexandrian Origen draws the pastoral lesson that we should "always investigate what opinion men may form of us," to "cut off the occasions" of evil report and multiply occasions of good. Cyril of Jerusalem adds that from the rejected synagogue Christ "built out of the Gentiles a second Holy Church, the Church of us Christians."
14:
The disciples report the popular guesses, and the witnesses weigh why men reached so honourably yet so wrongly. The grammatical-historical commentators trace each opinion to its root. Herod's own theory supplied "John the Baptist" (Matthew 14:2), and Henry notes that "those about him would be apt to say as he said," perhaps strengthened by a notion that the martyrs should rise first. The prophecy of Malachi (Malachi 4:5) and Elijah's wonder-working and translation — "himself, in his translation, the greatest miracle of all" — prompted "Elijah." Jeremiah was named, Lightfoot and Ellicott suggest, either because he was "the weeping prophet, and Christ was often in tears," or because God had set him "over the kingdoms and nations" (Jeremiah 1:10), which they thought agreed with their idea of Messiah. Robertson and Barnes note that all these opinions assumed Jesus to be one risen from the dead, sharing "a confused notion of the resurrection of the Messiah."

The Puritan Henry organises the consensus most fully. The opinions, he observes, are different — for "truth is one, but those who vary from that commonly vary one from another" — yet honourable, "the sober thoughts of those that followed him with love and wonder," and at the same time wholly false, "built upon mistakes, and wilful mistakes." Their root was the refusal to allow that "Jesus of Nazareth, one of their own country," could be so extraordinary a Person, so "they would say it was not he, but one of the old prophets." From this he draws his memorable warning: "it is possible for men to have good thoughts of Christ, and yet not right ones, a high opinion of him, and yet not high enough."

The Reformed Calvin and the Wesleyan Wesley concur that these were the verdicts of a people who esteemed antiquity above the present grace, "as if it were not possible for an excellent man to be produced in their own age," but one of the ancients must return to life. Henry adds that the disciples, tellingly, report only "such opinions as were wide of the truth," not the better guesses (Son of David, "that prophet") which some had ventured, so that the contrast with Peter's confession to follow might stand the sharper.
15:
"But who say ye that I am?" — and the witnesses mark the turn from hearsay to confession. The grammatical-historical commentators note the emphatic "ye": Ellicott and Robertson observe the pronoun is stressed, setting the disciples deliberately over against the crowd whose guesses they have just rehearsed. Barnes and Gill add that Jesus expected of those who had His intimacy a knowledge "more clear and distinct" than others possessed, and that the question is designed to draw from them a settled avowal rather than a report.

The Puritan Henry develops the demand most fully, on two grounds. First, the disciples "had, by their intimacy with Christ, greater advantages of getting knowledge than others"; and "it is justly expected that those who enjoy greater plenty of the means of knowledge and grace than others should have a more clear and distinct knowledge of the things of God." Second, they were themselves to teach others, "and therefore it was highly requisite that they should understand the truth themselves: ye that are to preach the gospel of the kingdom, what are your notions of him that sent you?" From this he draws a rule for the ministry: "ministers must be examined before they be sent forth, especially what their sentiments are of Christ, and who they say that he is; for how can they be owned as ministers of Christ that are either ignorant or erroneous concerning Christ?"

Henry then presses the question home upon every believer, making it a personal reckoning rather than a doctrinal exercise: "this is a question we should every one of us be frequently putting to ourselves — who do we say, what kind of one do we say, that the Lord Jesus is? Is he precious to us? Is he in our eyes the chief of ten thousand? Is he the Beloved of our souls?" — for "it is well or ill with us, according as our thoughts are right or wrong concerning Jesus Christ." The Reformed Calvin agrees the question demands a personal, settled confession, distinct from the floating opinions of the multitude, and prepares the way for the great answer that follows.
16:
Peter answers, "Thou art the Christ, the Son of the living God," and the witnesses across every age treat this as the church's confession in seed. The grammatical-historical commentators stress that Peter speaks "in the name of all the rest" (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott), as their mouthpiece and not by any peculiar primacy — Henry calls him "but foreman of the jury, to speak for the rest" — and that the confession joins the Messianic title to true deity. Ellicott and Robertson note "the living God" set in pointed contrast to the "dumb and dead idols," and Robertson observes that Peter's faith here becomes the rock on which the church is built in verse 18.

The Reformed Calvin presses the fulness of the confession: in it Peter "comprehends briefly the whole power of Christ," for to acknowledge Him as Son of the living God is to confess Him eternal and divine, the very fountain of life. Gill and Wesley keep close to the words: He is "the great Prophet, Priest, and King of the church, the true Messiah promised to the fathers," and "the Son of the living God, who has life in himself," in contrast to "the ghost of a dead man" which the people supposed Him to be.

The Alexandrian Origen, characteristically, makes the confession a ladder of knowledge. To believe Jesus is the Christ is, he suggests, "less than to know Him"; the apostles, sent out earlier, believed He was the Christ, "and afterwards as they made progress they knew Him to be so," so that Peter is here pronounced blessed "not only for that he says, Thou art the Christ, but much more for that he adds, the Son of the living God." Origen and Cyril of Jerusalem alike read "the living God" against the dead gods of the nations and the prophets' word "As I live, saith the Lord" (Ezekiel 33:11) — He alone has immortality and is the fount of life, "I am the life" (John 14:6). Across Greek, Latin, Reformed and modern voices there is no dissent on the substance: this is the saving confession of Christ's divine sonship.
17:
"Blessed art thou, Simon Bar-jona" — and the witnesses agree the blessing rests on revelation, not human discovery. The grammatical-historical commentators (Barnes, Ellicott, Robertson) and Gill explain the patronymic "Bar-jona" (son of Jonah, or of John) and insist that "flesh and blood" — human reason or instruction — could not disclose what "my Father which is in heaven" revealed. Robertson stresses the contrast carried by the emphatic "I also": the Father revealed one truth to Peter, "and I also tell you another," Jesus replying to him "both as a believer and as an apostle." Barnes notes the knowledge "sprang neither from nature nor from education, but from God."

The Puritan Henry draws out the doctrine in full. "The Christian religion is a revealed religion; it has its rise in heaven... not the learning of philosophers, nor the politics of statesmen." "Saving faith is the gift of God, and wherever it is, is wrought by him" (Philippians 1:29); and "the revealing of Christ to us and in us is a distinguishing token of God's good will, and a firm foundation of true happiness." He even discerns "something of pride and vain-glory" lurking in Peter's confidence, "a subtle sin," against which he warns that "our preference to others is no achievement of our own, but the free gift of God's grace" (1 Corinthians 4:7). The Reformed Calvin agrees the gifts are "ascribed to faith as if they had been a reward," a thing "we not unfrequently find in Scripture."

The Latin Jerome reads the title typologically: "Bar-jona," "the son of a dove," signifies the Holy Spirit, "whose son he shall also be called," by whose grace "that which flesh and blood could not reveal was revealed" — comparing Paul's "immediately I conferred not with flesh and blood" (Galatians 1:16); some, he allows, read it simply as "son of John." Cyril of Jerusalem sets the apostles above the prophets, "not inferior to Moses, nor second to the Prophets," for a servant of Christ "was caught up to the third heaven" (2 Corinthians 12:2). No voice dissents from the central claim: this faith is heaven-born, and its blessedness is the blessedness of those to whom the Father has revealed His Son.
18:
No verse in the chapter divides the witnesses more sharply, and the division runs straight along the great confessional fault-line over "Thou art Peter, and upon this rock I will build my church." Three readings of "the rock" stand in the tradition. The first, that Peter himself is the rock, is held — with very different conclusions — by the Latin and medieval stream and, stripped of any papal sense, by the grammatical-historical Barnes. The Gloss, drawing on the conciliar tradition and the later interpolated Cyril, builds on it "the primacy of Peter" and a single "Vicar of Christ... that the Church might have one principal head," lest "the bond of unity be broken." Barnes, by contrast, calls Peter the rock "the obvious meaning of the passage, and had it not been that the church of Rome has abused it... no other would have been sought for" — Peter honoured as the firm foundation-layer who first opened the gospel to Jew (Acts 2) and Gentile (Acts 10), but with "no such headship as Rome claims," still less infallible successors.

The second reading, that the rock is Christ Himself or Peter's confession, is the strong Protestant and patristic counter-current. Augustine is cited for his revised view — "the rock was Christ" (1 Corinthians 10:4), Peter "taking his name from this rock" — and Clarke, Ellicott and Wesley press it hard: Ellicott weighs the Greek petros ("stone") against petra ("rock") and the Old Testament's reservation of "Rock" for God (Deuteronomy 32:4), concluding the Rock is Christ "in the mystery of that union of the Divine and the Human" which Peter had confessed; Clarke insists Jesus "neither addressed Peter nor any other of the apostles" but Himself, confessed. Henry holds all three senses but anchors the church on "this truth that thou hast confessed... the main hinge on which the door of salvation turns."

Against Rome the Reformed and grammatical voices marshal a common arsenal: the apostles are the church's foundation together (Ephesians 2:20; Revelation 21:14); James, not Peter, presided at Jerusalem (Acts 15); Paul "withstood Peter to his face" (Galatians 2:11); and nothing is said of successors. Robertson and Henry add that "the gates of Hades" means death — the church is built on the Messiah whom death could not hold, and "Christ will rise and keep his church alive." The Alexandrian Origen democratises it entirely: "every one is a Rock who is an imitator of Christ," and what is promised to Peter holds "in regard to all" the perfect. Cyril of Jerusalem and Leo voice the high confidence in the one unconquered Church.
19:
The keys and the power to bind and loose continue the great controversy, and again the witnesses divide and converge along familiar lines. On the keys, almost all agree on their reference. The grammatical-historical Barnes, Lightfoot, Ellicott and Robertson, with the Reformed Calvin, read the key as the steward's symbol of office (Isaiah 22:22) and the scribe's "key of knowledge" (Luke 11:52), fulfilled when Peter "opened the door of faith" first to the Jews (Acts 2) and then to the Gentiles (Acts 10; Acts 15:7). Calvin calls the ministers "porters of the kingdom" who "carry its keys," and adds that the key "is placed in the hands of the ministers of the word," since "there is no other way in which the gate of life is opened to us than by the word of God." Barnes notes the key is "a symbol of superintendence... an emblem of power and authority" (Revelation 1:18; 3:7).

On binding and loosing the Protestant and grammatical voices are emphatic and unanimous against the sacerdotal reading. Drawing on rabbinic usage (the schools of Hillel and Shammai, who "loosed" and "bound" what the other forbade or allowed), Lightfoot, Barnes, Ellicott, Clarke and Robertson hold that "to bind" is to forbid and "to loose" to permit — a teacher's, not a priest's, function — concerning "things, not persons," and exercised as the apostles forbade and allowed Mosaic rites (Acts 15:20). Robertson warns it is "a violent leap in logic to claim power to forgive sins, to pronounce absolution, by reason of the technical rabbinical language that Jesus employed," and that "every preacher uses the keys of the kingdom when he proclaims the terms of salvation in Christ." Clarke and Lightfoot add that what was bound or loosed was "confirmed in heaven," the Holy Spirit directing the apostles as they decreed concerning the use of the law.

The great debate remains Petrine supremacy. Calvin devotes a long refutation to "the Roman Antichrist": the rock is Peter's faith, "the power to bind and to loose can no more be separated from the office of teaching than light or heat from the sun," and so the gift is "common to all the apostles" (Matthew 18:18); rank "is a different thing from power," and nothing was transmissible to successors. Ellicott ranks the Romish argument "on a level with that which sees the supremacy of the successors of St. Peter in the two great lights of Genesis 1:16." Against them the medieval Latin stream (which Gill reports) and Cyril of Jerusalem exalt Peter's singular receipt of the keys. The Alexandrian Origen again universalises: the keys are given to "every Peter," each virtue being "both the gate and the key of the gate." The Wesleyan Wesley splits the difference plainly — the keys were given to Peter "first," but "not to him alone, for they were equally given to all the apostles at the same time" (John 20:23).
20:
Jesus charges them to tell no man He is the Christ, and the witnesses ask why the confession just praised must now be hushed. The grammatical-historical commentators stress the strength of the charge — Barnes and Clarke note it is "strictly," even "severely" charged, some manuscripts reading "he threatened" — and read the reason in the politics of Messiahship: the people would inevitably take "Christ" in a political, kingly sense (Robertson), so that, had the disciples proclaimed it, men "would the more earnestly have sought to take and make him a king" (Wesley). The hour had not come to rouse "Jewish malice or Roman envy" (Clarke), and so He "chose rather to wait till his resurrection and ascension had set this truth in the clearest light." Ellicott discerns deeper motives: the homage of "excited crowds" was not "the faith which had its root in a deeper faith" that Christ sought, and "it tended to prevent the consummation to which He was now directing the thoughts of His disciples" — the zeal "to make him a king after their own fashion" (John 6:15) being what He deprecated and shunned.

The Reformed Calvin sets the silence within the coming conflict. The disciples were "not yet fully prepared to testify their faith, but, on the contrary, were so weak in faith that their confession of it would have exposed them to ridicule"; their earlier apostleship had been only temporary and preparatory, "to procure disciples for their Master," and ended when Christ began to preach. They must therefore remain silent "till others shall have acknowledged him to be the conqueror of death, and till he shall have endued them with increased firmness." The Wesleyan Wesley agrees the great proof was reserved for the resurrection, "that grand proof of it."

The Alexandrian Origen and the Latin Jerome supply the subtlest point, both insisting that to preach "Christ" without the cross is premature. Jerome distinguishes "preaching Christ" from "preaching Jesus the Christ" — the proper name from "the common title of dignity" — and concludes, "Then preach Me when I shall have suffered these things, for it will be of no avail that Christ be preached publicly when after a little time they shall see Him scourged and crucified." Origen says it would have been useless "to preach Him, and to be silent concerning His cross," for the full proclamation must wait until He could be proclaimed "as crucified, and who rose again from the dead"; he lingers too on the word "show," noting Christ "only began to show them" the mystery of the passion, and "afterwards, when they were more able to receive it, He shewed them more fully."
21:
"From that time" Jesus begins to show His coming death and resurrection, and the witnesses mark this as a deliberate new stage of teaching. The grammatical-historical commentators date and order it: Robertson notes it is "just a little over six months before the cross," the great confession making "this seem an appropriate time," and that Jesus plainly named the place (Jerusalem), the agents (elders, chief priests, scribes), the death, and the rising "on the third day, not on the fourth day, please observe." Ellicott observes the prediction came on the disciples "as something altogether new," for they had failed to take in the earlier veiled hints — "Destroy this temple" (John 2:19), the Son of Man "lifted up," the sign of Jonah — and argues that its very originality, and their failure to grasp the resurrection, tells against any theory of "a prophecy after the event."

The Puritan Henry gives the richest exposition. Christ "reveals his mind to his people gradually, and lets in light as they can bear it," waiting until they were "more ripe in knowledge and strong in faith"; "if they had not been well grounded in the belief of Christ's being the Son of God, it would have been a great shaking to their faith." He draws three purposes: to show the sufferings were no surprise but "the product of an eternal counsel and consent," agreed "between the Father and the Son from eternity"; to "rectify the mistakes which his disciples had imbibed concerning the external pomp and power of his kingdom"; and to prepare them "for the share of sorrow and fear which they must have in his sufferings," that "being fore-warned, they may be fore-armed." He stresses that Christ, who lived mostly in Galilee, "must die at Jerusalem, for there all the sacrifices were offered," and that the resurrection on the third day was added "to keep up their faith."

The Wesleyan voices note the shift from vague to express speech — "hitherto he had taught them only one point, that he was the Christ; from this time he taught them another, that Christ must through sufferings enter into his glory" (Wesley) — and with the Alexandrian Origen dwell on the divine necessity (dei, "must"). Origen reads the going up to Jerusalem and the making of "the first-fruits of the resurrection" as the appointed pattern, since "it cannot be that a prophet perish out of Jerusalem," and adds, with his usual reserve about the word "show," that Christ "altogether completed that which He began to show to His disciples about the necessity of His suffering."
22:
Peter takes Him aside and rebukes Him — "Be it far from thee, Lord" — and the witnesses weigh how so loving a protest could be so wrong. The grammatical-historical commentators recover the words and the gesture. Barnes, Ellicott, Clarke and Robertson explain that the Greek is an abbreviated prayer, "(God be) merciful to thee," better rendered "pity thyself" or "spare thyself," and that Peter "took him" aside familiarly. Robertson, citing Bengel, notes Peter "acted with greater familiarity after the token of acknowledgment had been given," and, citing Bruce, that "Peter here appears in a new character; a minute ago speaking under inspiration from heaven, now under inspiration from the opposite quarter." Ellicott observes that Peter's mind "dwelt on the former, not the latter part of the prediction": the death was "plain and terrible to him, for he failed to grasp the idea of the resurrection," and "personal love for his Lord, his own desire to share in the glory which that promise had implied, were united in his refusal."

The fathers measure the fault charitably but firmly. The Latin Jerome attributes it to Peter's "too hot a zeal, and a very great affection towards the Lord," who "would not have that his confession destroyed," and — pointedly — declares that "this error of the Apostle, proceeding from the warmth of his affection, will never seem to me a suggestion of the devil." Hilary likewise will not impute "the name of Satan, and the sin of being an offence," to Peter himself "after those so great declarations of blessedness," but to the temptation suggested through him. The Antiochene Chrysostom explains that Peter, having received "no revelation concerning these things," and "estimating the things of Christ by human and earthly principles, judged it mean and unworthy of Him that He should suffer."

The Reformed Calvin is the sharpest. Peter's zeal is real but presumptuous, advising Christ "as if he had been deficient in prudence or self-command"; men, "hurried on by inconsiderate zeal, do not hesitate to pass judgment on God himself, according to their own fancy," and "we ought to yield greater deference to the opinion of Christ than to the zeal of Peter." He warns that "there is no beast more furious than the wisdom of the flesh," even when, as here, "its intention was pious, or at least it looked well." The Alexandrian Origen frames it theologically — Peter, forgetting "that the Son of the living God does nothing worthy of blame," treated Him "as one who needed a propitiation" — and Cyril of Jerusalem turns the scene to the willing innocence of the sinless Sufferer who, "if any dissuading Him say even now, Be it far from Thee, Lord, will say again, Get thee behind Me, Satan."
23:
"Get thee behind me, Satan" — the sternest rebuke Christ gives any disciple, and the witnesses labour to take its measure. The grammatical-historical commentators soften and define it. Barnes and Clarke insist Satan here bears its Hebrew sense of "adversary" — "an opposer, one that stands in the way" — and that Jesus "did not mean to apply this term to Peter as signifying that he was the devil," the rebuke being real yet less harsh than the English conveys: "your advice and wishes are in my way." Clarke renders it "get behind me, thou adversary," judging this "takes away that harshness which before appeared in our Lord's words." Robertson, with Mark's detail that He "turned about and looked on his disciples," reads the reversal: "just before Peter played the part of a rock in the noble confession... now he is playing the part of Satan and is ordered to the rear," become "Satan's catspaw, in ignorance, surely, but none the less really." Ellicott hears "a renewal of the same temptation" of the wilderness — a crown without a cross — and adds a long application to the Papacy's "struggle for temporal power" as the great instance of one who "savoured not the things that be of God, but those that be of man."

The Alexandrian Origen draws the finest distinction in the tradition. To the devil in the wilderness Christ said "get thee hence," but to Peter "get thee behind me" — "for to be behind Jesus is a good thing," as in "Come ye behind Me, and I will make you fishers of men" (Matthew 4:19) — so the word calls Peter "to follow," not to damnation, "as to one who through ignorance was ceasing to walk after Christ." He wrestles too with how the Lord could be "offended" (a scandalon) when "great peace have they that love thy law, and there is no offence to them" (Psalm 119:165), concluding that "not only is Jesus not offended, but neither is any man who is perfect in the love of God," and yet "he who does or speaks any thing of the nature of an offence may be an offence even to one who is incapable of being offended."

The Reformed Calvin again reads it as a warning against "the wisdom of the flesh," which Christ "bruises with an iron hammer," teaching "that it is only from the word of God that we ought to be wise"; in restraining Peter "in the person of one man he intended to restrain all from gratifying their own passions." The Wesleyan Wesley notes the singular sharpness was needful "for the pride of Peter's heart, puffed up with the commendation lately given him," and that to whisper "favour thyself" is "to act the very part of Satan." Cyril of Jerusalem closes with the willing innocence of the One who "did no sin," yet answered every dissuader, "Get thee behind Me, Satan."
24:
"If any man will come after me, let him deny himself, and take up his cross, and follow me." The witnesses receive this as the law of discipleship and dwell on each clause. The Antiochene Chrysostom anchors the consensus on its freedom. Christ "does not speak of it as compulsory; He says not, Though ye will not, yet must ye suffer this, but, If any man will" — "for he who leaves his auditor at liberty attracts him the more, whereas he that uses violence oftentimes hinders him." He proposes it "in common to the whole world: if woman, if man, if king, if free, if slave," and explains self-denial by a vivid analogy: as a man may disown a brother or servant and "neither succour nor befriend him" though he see him beaten, so "He would have us deny our body, and whether it be beaten or afflicted in any other way, not to spare it" — even unto "death the most shameful, even that of the cross."

The Latin fathers deepen the inwardness. Gregory the Great explains that "he denies himself whosoever is changed for the better, and begins to be what he was not, and ceases to be what he was"; and he warns that "our very virtues are beset with faults" — vainglory may attend abstinence, "false affection" may attend compassion — which only "follow me" can purge. Jerome and Hilary read cross-bearing as being "crucified to the world," following the crucified Lord "if not in deed, yet in will." The Alexandrian Origen ties self-denial to dying with Christ — "I live, yet no longer I, but Christ liveth in me" was "the voice of one denying himself" (Galatians 2:20) — and specifically to the cross, "in order that all who die unto sin may die to it in no other way than by the way of the cross" (Galatians 6:14).

The Puritan Henry organises the terms for the ordinary believer. Discipleship is to "come after" Christ, "not one that prescribes to him, as Peter now undertook to do"; self-denial is "the strait gate and the narrow way," to be practised "for Christ, and for our brethren, and for ourselves"; and the cross is "that which Infinite Wisdom has appointed for us," lying in our way, to be taken up "out of our way" and "improved to some good advantage" — "this is an evil, and I will bear it, because it shall work for my good." He quotes Tillotson that one who "cannot take up the resolution to live a saint" is "never likely to die a martyr." Gill notes the timeliness: having owned Himself Messiah, Christ at once teaches them to expect not riches but the cross.
25:
The paradox — "whosoever will save his life shall lose it; and whosoever will lose his life for my sake shall find it" — is received by the witnesses with one voice, differing only in framing. The grammatical-historical and Reformed commentators set the two lives in eternal scales: Barnes, Clarke, Ellicott and Calvin all read the saving and losing across the boundary of death, so that "he that forsakes Christ to preserve a temporal life and avoid a temporal death will certainly come short of eternal life," while "he that is content to lose his life for owning Christ shall find it" in a better world, "infinitely to his advantage."

The Puritan Henry weighs "the weight of that eternity which depends upon our present choice," setting "life and death, good and evil, the blessing and the curse" before us. On the one hand, "there cannot be a fairer pretence for apostasy than saving the life by it, so cogent is the law of self-preservation; and yet even that is folly, for it will prove in the end self-destruction: the life saved is but for a moment... but the life lost is everlasting." On the other, the constancy that loses life finds it again, for "Christ's holy religion is handed down to us, sealed with the blood of thousands, that have despised their lives" when these stood "in competition with their duty and the testimony of Jesus" (Revelation 20:4); an assurance of the life they should find "hath enabled them to triumph over death, to go smiling to a scaffold, and stand singing at a stake."

The Alexandrian Origen offers a double reading consistent with his exposition of verse 24. If self-denial there means "the death of the body," then "losing the life" is literal martyrdom — "if any lover of this present life spares his life, fearing to die... he seeking in this way to save his life shall lose it, estranging it from life eternal"; but if it means "mortifying the propensities of the flesh," then to lose the life "signifies to give up carnal pleasures." Either way, the loss is "for Christ's sake," and so "he shall the more save it for life eternal." Cyril of Jerusalem and Wesley keep the plain edge: the life hazarded for Christ is repaid beyond all measure, and the bargain of apostasy, however prudent it looks, is the worst a man can make.
26:
"What is a man profited, if he shall gain the whole world, and lose his own soul?" The witnesses make this the great weighing of the soul against all things, and they are unanimous on its worth. The grammatical-historical commentators note that the word for "soul" (psyche) is the same rendered "life" in verse 25, "for the soul is the life" (Genesis 2:7); so the saying rises from the common truism that "whatever a man gets, if he lose his life, it will do him no good, he cannot enjoy his gains," to the higher truth of "the soul as immortal, and a loss of it beyond death, which cannot be compensated by the gain of the whole world" (Barnes, Ellicott, Robertson).

The Puritan Henry expounds it in four propositions. Every man has a soul of his own — "the spiritual and immortal part of man, which thinks and reasons," ours "not in respect of dominion (for all souls are mine, saith God), but in respect of nearness and concern." The soul can be lost, "eternally separated from all good," when "it is separated from the favour of God, and sunk under his wrath." If lost, it is "of the sinner's own losing, for he does that which is certainly destroying to it... the sinner dies because he will die; his blood is on his own head." And "one soul is worth more than all the world" — "here is the whole world set in the scale against one soul, and Tekel written upon it" (Daniel 5:27), "weighed in the balance and found too light to weigh it down; and this is Christ's judgment upon the matter, and he is a competent Judge."

The fathers add the note of irrecoverable exchange. The Antiochene Chrysostom and the Latin Gregory, Jerome, Remigius and Raban press the "exchange" (the antallagma): nothing a man possesses — "not the whole world" — can be given in ransom for a soul once lost, for the soul, once forfeited, is beyond all purchase. The Alexandrian Origen presses the metaphysics of the soul's value, that what bears the image of God cannot be priced against perishable things; and the Reformed Calvin keeps the practical edge — those who barter the soul for the world make the most ruinous of all bargains, gaining what they cannot keep and losing what they cannot recover.
27:
"For the Son of man shall come in the glory of his Father with his angels; and then he shall reward every man according to his works." The witnesses read this as the ground of the foregoing call: the cross is bearable because the glory is sure. The grammatical-historical and Reformed commentators (Barnes, Ellicott, Clarke, Calvin) take it of the final advent in glory, when judgment will be rendered "according to works" — not, the Reformed are careful to add, as the meritorious cause of salvation, but as its evidence and measure. Calvin reads it as the consolation that steadies the disciples after the hard word of the cross: "as there is nothing to be seen in the cross but the weakness of the flesh, till we come to his resurrection... our faith will find no encouragement," so the certain prospect of the coming kingdom is held out to support them, and "all ministers of the Word ought to be careful that the glory of his resurrection should always be exhibited in connection with the ignominy of his death."

The Wesleyan Wesley keeps the plain doctrine of reward and accountability, and Gill expounds the "glory of his Father" as the divine majesty in which the Son will appear, "attended by the holy angels" as the ministers of judgment. The verse, all agree, sets the believer's present self-denial within the prospect of an open and certain vindication: what is lost for Christ now will be more than repaid then, when He comes not "in secret" as a physician, but "with much pomp" as the judge.

The Alexandrian Origen, as so often, turns the language toward the inner life, reading the coming "in glory" and the reward "according to works" in moral and spiritual terms, the soul's deeds determining its portion and its progress toward the vision of God. Cyril of Jerusalem gathers the apostolic witness to the Son's session "at the right hand of power," citing Peter, Paul to the Romans, and the letters to the Ephesians and Colossians, to confirm that the Sufferer of these verses is none other than the coming Judge who appears "in the glory of his Father." Across the schools the note is single: the certainty of that glory is what makes the demand to deny self, take up the cross, and lose one's life not a counsel of despair but the surest of investments.
28:
"There be some standing here, which shall not taste of death, till they see the Son of man coming in his kingdom." This is the chapter's second great crux, and the witnesses spread across a range of fulfilments rather than dividing neatly into two camps. The dominant patristic and traditional reading — the one the very next chapter invites — is the Transfiguration, since the Synoptists all place it immediately after this saying; Robertson lists it first among the possibilities, and the fathers generally read the saying as fulfilled when Peter, James and John saw His glory on the mount.

The Reformed Calvin gives the most developed alternative. He animates the disciples with "an intermediate period": before they come to die, "you will see with your eyes that kingdom of God." By the coming "in his kingdom" he understands "the manifestation of heavenly glory which Christ began to make at his resurrection, and which he afterwards made more fully by sending the Holy Spirit, and by the performance of miracles" — the disciples tasting "the newness of the heavenly life" when they perceived Him "sitting at the right hand of the Father." He scorns as "ridiculous" the notion, held by some, that the saying refers to John outliving the rest. The Wesleyan Wesley, with Gill, leans toward a further horizon: the Messiah "coming to set up his mediatorial kingdom, with great power and glory, by the increase of his Church, and the destruction of the temple, city, and polity of the Jews" — Pentecost issuing in the fall of Jerusalem.

The grammatical-historical Robertson holds the whole range open with deliberate reserve: "we do not know" whether the Transfiguration, the Resurrection, the great Day of Pentecost, the Destruction of Jerusalem, or the Second Coming is meant — "only that Jesus was certain of his final victory which would be typified and symbolized in various ways" — warning that this apocalyptic symbolism "does not dominate his teaching," for "the kingdom of God was already in the hearts of men." The Alexandrian Origen reads it inwardly, of the soul that, not tasting death, comes to behold the kingdom in the Word, linking it to "he that loseth his life for my sake shall find it." Cyril of Jerusalem anchors it in the session at "the right hand of power," gathering the apostolic witnesses to the risen Christ. Across the schools the shared conviction is firm even where the referent is disputed: the kingdom would be seen, in power, within that generation's sight.
